title: Couscous royale
subtitle: Moroccan couscous with lamb, chicken, and merguez
excerpt: The grand version of Friday couscous: steamed couscous served with a rich, saffron-and-vegetable broth, falling-tender braised lamb, chicken pieces, and merguez sausages. Spiced with ras el hanout and sweetened with turnip and carrot, with a bowl of harissa on the side. The centre-piece of the Moroccan table.
sourceNotes: Couscous royale is the most elaborate version of the Moroccan Friday couscous, a dish with the significance and ritual of a Sunday roast. Paula Wolfert's Couscous and Other Good Food from Morocco (1973) remains the primary English-language authority on its preparation. 'Royale' denotes the inclusion of multiple proteins alongside the vegetable broth.

content.text: The couscous is the foundation, not the garnish. It should be light, separate, and fragrant from butter or olive oil. If using instant couscous, the simplest method is to pour boiling salted water over it (1:1 ratio), cover for 5 minutes, then fluff with a fork and work in butter. For a more authentic result, steam the couscous in a couscoussier over the simmering broth in two rounds.

The broth is where the flavour lives. It needs time, at least 1 hour of simmering with the lamb and vegetables. The merguez are cooked separately in a dry pan at the end; they are too fatty to cook in the broth without it becoming greasy.

In a large pot, combine the {{lamb-shoulder}}, {{chicken-thigh}}, {{onion}}, {{ras-el-hanout}}, {{cinnamon-ground}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, saffron water, and {{tinned-tomatoes}} with 1.5 litres of water. Bring to a boil, skim the foam, then reduce to a steady simmer. Cook for 1 hour. Add the {{carrot}} and cook for 15 minutes. Add the {{courgette}} and drained {{chickpeas}} and cook for a further 15 minutes. The broth should be fragrant and the lamb falling from the bone.

Cook the {{merguez}} in a dry hot pan for 6 to 8 minutes, turning, until cooked through and browned all over. Meanwhile, put the {{couscous}} in a large bowl and pour over 500 ml of boiling water from the broth. Cover and leave for 5 minutes. Fluff with a fork, work in the {{unsalted-butter}}, and season with salt. To serve: mound the couscous in the centre of a large platter, arrange the meat, sausages, and vegetables around or on top, and ladle enough broth over to moisten. Serve the remaining broth and {{harissa}} separately.

items.fix: Add a little more boiling water and work in the butter very thoroughly with your fingers, lifting and separating the grains. The butter coats each grain
items.cause: Not enough liquid or not enough butter worked in

items.fix: The broth needs at least 90 minutes of simmering with the meat. If it tastes thin, continue reducing uncovered
items.cause: Insufficient simmering time or too much water

items.fix: Continue cooking in 15-minute increments. Shoulder is done when the meat pulls away from the bone easily
items.cause: Bone-in pieces need longer on the bone

content.text: Friday couscous is the meal of the Moroccan week. In Morocco, couscous is served only on Fridays in most households. It is the family meal, the homecoming meal, the meal that punctuates the week. Couscous royale is the celebratory version, expanded for guests, with more meats and a more elaborate presentation. In France, where North African immigration is significant, couscous royale has become one of the most popular restaurant dishes in the country. In Britain it is found in Moroccan restaurants and increasingly in domestic cooking.
